text.content:
Table of Contents
  • Act 1 at a Glance
    • The Nine Beats
  • Threads Running Through Act 1
    • Dreyfus: The Dog That Never Leaves
    • Grafvitnir: The Bound Enemy (GM Secret)
    • Gragnir: The Ash-Walker’s Arc
    • Campaign Currencies in Act 1
  • Level Progression
  • Act 1 Encounter Budget
    • Combat Encounters
    • Montage Tests
    • Negotiation Encounters
  • Grafvitnir Pressure Check (Act 1 Scale)
  • What Act 1 Establishes

The heroes are summoned, forged together by hardship, and sent on a desperate quest to find the one person who might save Svellheim from eternal winter.

Act 1 at a Glance

Levels: 1 → 3
Sessions: 10–16 (adjust pacing to table)
Tone: Survival, paranoia, desperate hope, road-fellowship
Arc: Strangers become companions; the crisis is confirmed as existential

The Nine Beats

Beat Name Sessions Summary
1 The Konungs-ferð ½ The summons reaches the PCs' homes. They are chosen—or volunteered.
2 The Road to Hrafnborg ½–1 Travel montage. PCs meet on the road. Draugr ambush at a graveyard ruin.
3 The Quiet Summons 1 Polite custody in Hrafnborg. Harald reveals the crisis and the mission: find Kaelen. Gragnir names the first step: find Lew. Dreyfus (the hound) listens.
4 The Road to Stairwatch 1–2 Sneaking out of Hrafnborg. 200 miles / ~10 days. Two Hard montage tests, combat (Frost-Draugr), negotiation (Chainwarden Toll), POIs.
5 Finding Lew 1–2 Stairwatch. Negotiation with Lew. Local tasks. The false eye is revealed. Lew agrees to guide them.
6 The Great Green 2–3 160 miles (Ashen Woods) or 125 miles (Bone Fields). Route choice. Three Hard montage rounds, 2 combats, POIs, Grafvitnir network pressure.
7 The Reluctant Hearth 1–2 Kaelen’s hidden grove. Very Hard negotiation. She comes—with conditions or after an attack forces her hand.
8 The Long Road Home 2–3 220 miles / ~12 days. Three Moderate montage rounds, negotiation (Greyfen Holdfast), Gragnir reveals Dreyfus’s master.
9 The Siege of Hrafnborg 1–2 Hrafnborg is besieged by undead. Montage test to breach the siege. The Ever-Embers are failing because Yggdrasil is dying. The party must journey to the Emberwell. Act 1 ends; Act 2 begins.

Threads Running Through Act 1

Dreyfus: The Dog That Never Leaves

Dreyfus appears first as High King Harald’s grey hound in Beat 3. He is present for every key conversation in Hrafnborg. He does not eat. He does not sleep. He watches mouths more than hands.

Dreyfus never leaves the King’s side. Throughout Act 1—and all of Act 2—he remains in dog form beside Harald, listening to every war council, every messenger’s report, every whispered conversation in the King’s chambers. He relays intelligence to Grafvitnir through Wyrd-channels: root-whispers that travel through Yggdrasil’s dying network, received by the Grave-Wolf’s other servants and passed to Pale Maw operatives in the field.

The party never sees Dreyfus on the road. The interference they encounter comes from Grafvitnir’s network—Pale Maw agents, corrupted officials, informant chains—all acting on intelligence that Dreyfus provides from Hrafnborg.

Beat 3: Dreyfus hears the mission.
Beats 4–7: The Grafvitnir Pressure Check activates. Pale Maw agents, corrupted informants, and Wyrd-touched creatures create complications—acting on intelligence relayed from Hrafnborg. The PCs don’t know the source.
Beat 8: Gragnir reveals the truth: Dreyfus is not a dog. He is a shape-walker, an agent of Grafvitnir—and he has been sitting beside Harald this entire time. The party cannot confront him from over two hundred miles away.
Beat 9: The party returns to Hrafnborg knowing the King’s hound is the enemy’s spy. Dreyfus is still there, still watching. This tension carries into Act 2.
End of Act 2: Dreyfus finally reveals himself and attacks Harald directly.

Grafvitnir: The Bound Enemy (GM Secret)

⚠ Director Secret: The Grave-Wolf

Grafvitnir is the campaign’s true antagonist—not Níðhöggr (which is entropy, mindless gnawing), but a being of intelligent malice imprisoned in the deepest root-chamber of Yggdrasil.

Grafvitnir was bound during the First Kindling, when the original Ember-Keepers sealed the root-chamber and lit the Ever-Embers as both warmth-anchors and prison-locks. As Fimbulwinter weakens Yggdrasil, Grafvitnir’s prison thins. Every Ever-Ember that dies is a lock that opens.

Dreyfus is Grafvitnir’s shape-walker—a fragment of will given form and sent into the world to watch, listen, and hasten the tree’s death. He remains at Harald’s side because that is where the most valuable intelligence flows: troop movements, supply routes, diplomatic messages, and the PCs' mission details. He relays this through Wyrd-channels—root-whispers carried along Yggdrasil’s dying network to Grafvitnir’s other agents.

Dreyfus cannot directly harm those under guest-right (he is still Wyrd-bound by the rules of the world he wants to destroy). He can only observe and report. Grafvitnir’s other servants—Pale Maw cultists, corrupted officials, Wyrd-touched creatures—act on the intelligence he provides.

This is not revealed until Beat 8. Until then, the PCs should attribute complications to the Pale Maw, bandits, or bad luck. When they learn the truth, the horror is that the spy is back in Hrafnborg with the King and they can do nothing about it from the road. This tension drives the return journey and carries into Act 2, culminating when Dreyfus finally reveals himself and attacks Harald.

Gragnir: The Ash-Walker’s Arc

Gragnir is the campaign’s Gandalf/Allanon figure—he provides direction, not answers.

Beat 3: Appears in Harald’s sitting room. Names Lew as the key to finding Kaelen.
Beat 5: Gragnir and Lew share a history (the false eye is Gragnir’s gift). This emerges through Lew’s stories, not exposition.
Beats 6–7: Gragnir may appear at a critical moment—a fork in the road, a dire combat, a moral crossroads—but he does not stay.
Beat 8: Gragnir meets the full party on the return road and delivers the truth about Dreyfus and Grafvitnir. This is the campaign’s first major lore reveal. The horror: the spy is back in Hrafnborg and they can do nothing from over two hundred miles away.

Campaign Currencies in Act 1

Heat (fuel, food, warmth): the dominant scarcity. Every travel montage costs Heat. Losing Heat means cold, exhaustion, and death.
Weight (tools, weapons, transport): secondary. Equipment damage on failed montages, repair costs in settlements, trade goods for negotiation.
Word (reputation, oaths, legal standing): social currency. Forged papers, faction favors, Chainwarden warrants, and the Konungs-ferð oath itself are all measured in Word.

Level Progression

Beat Milestone Level
1–2 Survive the road and the draugr ambush 1
3–4 Accept the mission and reach Stairwatch 2
5–7 Find Lew and Kaelen; the party is forged 3
8–9 Return to besieged Hrafnborg; the truth revealed 3 (entering Act 2 at 4)

Act 1 Encounter Budget

Combat Encounters

Beat Encounter Difficulty Notes
2 Draugr ambush at the graveyard ruin Moderate First real fight. Teach the system.
4 Frost-Draugr on the Mountain Road (2 Frost-Draugr + 3 Grave-Wisps) Moderate Between the two montage tests. Full stat blocks in beat file.
5 Stairwatch complications (bar fight, thugs, Chainwarden confrontation) Easy–Moderate Social with teeth. Can be avoided.
6 Rot-Beasts at the Treeline (2 Rot-Beasts + 1 Vine Strangler) Moderate Ashen Woods route. Terrain matters—charcoal trunks, ash drifts, hidden roots.
6 Barrow-Draugr Ambush (3 Barrow-Draugr + 1 Grave-Singer) Moderate Bone Fields alternate route. Substitutes for Rot-Beasts if PCs take this path.
6 Rootgnaw Brood (4 Grubs + 1 Queen Boss L3) Hard Near the Green Heart boundary. Both routes converge here. Living roots as terrain.
7 Green Heart guardians (dryads, Rot-warped landvaettir) Hard Triggered on negotiation failure OR as separate encounter.
8 Return road encounters (draugr patrols, blizzard hazards) Moderate–Hard Escalation. The dead are organized and converging on Hrafnborg.
9 Siege breakthrough Hard Climactic Act 1 combat. Getting through the undead to Hrafnborg’s walls.

Montage Tests

Beat Montage Difficulty
2 The road to Hrafnborg Easy
4 Hrafnborg to Stairwatch — TWO separate Hard montages (200mi, ~10 days) Hard
6 Stairwatch to Green Heart — 3 rounds (160mi Ashen Woods / 125mi Bone Fields) Hard
8 Green Heart to Hrafnborg — 3 rounds (220mi, ~12 days, worsening weather) Moderate
9 Breaching the siege of Hrafnborg Hard

Negotiation Encounters

Beat Negotiation Difficulty
4 Chainwarden Toll at Greypass (Warden-Sergeant Eirik) Moderate (Interest 5, Patience 4)
5 Bargaining with Lew Moderate (Interest 6, Patience 5)
7 Convincing Kaelen to return Very Hard (Interest 5, Patience 4, with pitfalls)
8 Greyfen Holdfast (Thyra Blackhand, refugee camp) Moderate (Interest 5, Patience 4)

Grafvitnir Pressure Check (Act 1 Scale)

After each travel montage or major faction scene (starting in Beat 4), make a secret d6 check. This represents Grafvitnir’s network acting on intelligence Dreyfus relays from Hrafnborg—not Dreyfus himself, who never leaves the King’s side.

Roll Result
1–3 The network does not act this beat. Intelligence is still flowing, but Grafvitnir’s agents haven’t caught up or aren’t in position.
4–5 Network interference: Pale Maw agents spoil rations, tip off a Chainwarden patrol, plant a rumor that poisons a negotiation, or a stranger on the road asks too many questions about where the PCs are going.
6 Major intervention: Pale Maw operatives attempt a direct interception—an ambush, a planted agent posing as a traveller, or intelligence passed to local authorities that puts the PCs' cover at risk.

Escalation: Starting in Beat 6, change the scale: 1–2 = no action, 3–5 = network interference, 6 = major intervention.

Starting in Beat 8, change again: 1 = no action, 2–4 = network interference, 5–6 = major intervention.

What Act 1 Establishes

By the end of Act 1, the players should know:

  • Svellheim is dying. The Ever-Embers are failing because Yggdrasil itself is dying.

  • An ancient evil—Grafvitnir, the Grave-Wolf—is imprisoned in Yggdrasil’s roots and its prison is weakening as the tree withers.

  • Dreyfus was never a dog. He is Grafvitnir’s agent—and he is still at Harald’s side, listening to every word. The King doesn’t know.

  • The party must journey to the Emberwell and renew the spark of life in Yggdrasil. This is their Act 2 quest.

  • Kaelen carries the knowledge of the old rites. Lew can find the way. The party provides the will.

  • Every faction has its own agenda, and the heroes cannot trust institutions—only each other.

Design Goals

  • Replace external-setting cosmology with Svellheim’s: Yggdrasil as infrastructure, Wyrd as Word made inevitable (oath-infrastructure), the Rot as infrastructure failure.
  • Keep pressure character-driven (Grafvitnir's network, fed by Dreyfus at the king's side), not clock-driven.
  • Keep every phase playable with Draw Steel’s strengths: tactical combat, Negotiations, Montage Tests, tiered consequences.
  • Preserve the campaign’s central moral blade: the world can be saved, but not for free.

The Belts and Choke-Points (Campaign Region)

Svellheim is divided into three survivability zones ("Belts"). Restore the Flame is set three years into Fimbulwinter, with play focused on the Coast and the perilous routes inland.

Use borders as logistics, not map lines: who controls the flow of Heat, Weight, and Word.

  • Coastal Belt: The Splintered Fjords (Hrafnborg)
    • Refugee city where rationing is law.
    • Power center for the Chainwardens (Word for profit; tariffs and contracts).
  • Interior Belt: the routes that still (sometimes) work
    • The Lantern Road (Word + Weight): wayfinders, news, and transport.
    • The Red-Rivet Compact (Weight): smiths, tools, iron as survival.
    • The Ashwright Kins (Heat): peat, oil, coal, and warmth monopolies.
    • The Quiet Wardens (Word): burial rites, Barrow-Law, and keeping the dead down.
  • Pressure from other powers
    • The Blackwater Vow (Word + Weight): martial law, raids, consolidation by force.
    • The Loom-Circle (Word): seidr, healing, and bargains that feel like fate.
    • The Pale Maw (Void): doomsday cult sabotage—"mercy" through ending.

The Emberwell (Campaign Keystone)

The Emberwell is not a public location on a map. It is a deep infrastructure-knot where Yggdrasil’s circulation still almost holds.

Use existing geography:

  • The Throat of the World is the visible landmark (fire-and-ice country).
  • The Steam-Oasis is the survivable shell and staging ground.
  • The Emberwell is the sealed chamber below—half geothermal engine, half root-knot.

What people believe:

  • The Emberwell is "where warmth comes from," or "where the world’s last hearth still breathes."
  • The Need-Fire is an old rite that might help.

What is true (GM):

  • The Emberwell is a failing junction in Yggdrasil’s system.
  • The system cannot be restarted without a price paid in Word made flesh.

Optional (GM): the Ember-Keepers (a secret society) chase the same goal—reigniting the Sun—and their experiments with Soul-Fuel are either a rival solution or a terrible shortcut.

The Unseen Enemy (Name Spoken in Hushes)

The name people use: The Rootgnaw

Everyone has a different story. Nobody has proof.

  • Some say it is a worm in the world’s bones.
  • Some say it is a wolf chewing the sun’s leash.
  • Some say it is the dead scratching from below.

The Rootgnaw is an ephemeral enemy: never seen, never directly fought.

The truth (GM): Níðhöggr

In your cosmology, Níðhöggr gnaws the roots and anchor points of reality.

In play, heroes fight:

  • the Rot’s manifestations,
  • the Rootgnaw’s brood (wights, beasts, landvaettir gone wrong),
  • and the humans who weaponize collapse.

Campaign Antagonists

The Pale Maw (Faction)

The Pale Maw are a doomsday cult whose mercy is annihilation.

They are not cosmic gods. They are people who have decided:

  • law is a lie,
  • warmth is cruelty rationed,
  • and silence is peace.

They sabotage Heat, Weight, and Word to speed the end.

Dreyfus (The Hound)

Dreyfus is a shape-walker bound to Grafvitnir. He never leaves the King’s side—he IS the King’s grey hound, lying by the fire, listening to every whispered plan. He is the campaign’s intelligence source, not its field agent:

  • he overhears the party’s plans in Hrafnborg,
  • he relays intelligence via Wyrd-channels (root-whispers through Yggdrasil’s dying network),
  • Grafvitnir’s other agents (Pale Maw cultists, corrupted officials, Wyrd-touched creatures) act on that intelligence in the field.

Table-facing introduction: Dreyfus appears only as the King’s hound in the room—quiet, listening, and present for secrets. He never transforms or leaves while the party is in Hrafnborg.

The network does the work: Grafvitnir’s agents use warrants, forged seals, bribed Chainwardens, and Wyrd-touched carrion birds (silver-eyed Valravn) to pressure the party on the road. Dreyfus never leaves Hrafnborg until the end of Act 2, when he reveals himself and attacks Harald.

Key NPCs

High King Harald

Role: Desperate monarch, tactical pragmatist, oathbound ruler

Appearance: Greying, ink-stained hands, wearing simple clothes (no crown in private). Looks older than his legend suggests.

Demeanor: Blunt, exhausted, but still commanding.

Motivation: Save Hrafnborg. Save Svellheim. Protect his people at any cost—even if that cost is his own legitimacy.

High King Harald — Draw Steel NPC

Level 7 Brute Leader · EV 28 · Humanoid, Hearthborn, King

Stamina: 160Speed: 6 (walk)Size: M
Stability: 2Free Strike: 7

Characteristics: Might +5 · Agility +2 · Reason +2 · Intuition +3 · Presence +4

Features
  • End Effect: Harald can take 5 damage to immediately end one “save ends” effect on himself.
  • Supernatural Insight: Harald ignores supernatural concealment.
Signature Abilities
  • Hit and Run (Action, Melee, Might): Staying in constant motion helps you slip out of reach after a brutal assault. Deals 2/5/7 + Might damage; shift 1 after the attack. Tier 3: target is slowed (save ends).
  • Parry (Triggered, Signature): When hit, Harald shifts 1 square and takes half damage; reduces potency by 1.
Heroic Abilities
  • Your Entrails Are Your Extrails! (3 Rage, Melee, Might): 3/5/8 + Might damage; target is bleeding (save ends). While bleeding, the target takes 5 (Might) damage at the end of each of Harald’s turns.
  • En Garde! (5 Rage, Melee, Agility): High-damage dueling strike; triggers an exchange of free strikes with the target.

Negotiation: Interest 7 · Patience 4

Director Notes: Run Harald as a war-king: decisive, practical, and unsentimental. In battle, he is the hearth made iron—an old fury disciplined into law. In Phase 1, Harald is not fighting—he’s confessing. If Harald enters combat, the situation has catastrophically collapsed.

Key Trait: Harald does not lie. He omits, he redirects, but he does not lie.

Gragnir, the Ash-Walker

Role: Wandering seidr-worker, fragment-keeper, cryptic guide (Allanon-role)

Appearance: Tall, gaunt, ash-stained hands, smoke-grey furs, smells of burning peat.

Demeanor: Sardonic, blunt. Never lies, but never tells the whole truth.

Motivation: Keep the world’s infrastructure from total collapse—but through the PCs’ hands, not his own.

Gragnir, Ash-Walker — Draw Steel NPC

Level 10 Controller Elite · EV 48 · Humanoid, Druid

Stamina: 220Speed: 7 (walk, climb)Size: M
Free Strike: 10

Characteristics: Might +0 · Agility +2 · Reason +4 · Intuition +5 · Presence +3

Features
  • End Effect: Gragnir can take 5 damage to immediately end one “save ends” effect on himself.
  • Supernatural Insight: Gragnir ignores supernatural concealment.
Signature Ability
  • The Green Within, the Green Without (Action, Ranged 10, Reason): Whipping vines erupt from a foe’s body to grasp at another close by. Deals 2/5/7 + Reason damage; slide one creature within 10 squares of the target up to 2 squares.
Heroic Abilities
  • Thorn Cage (11 Essence, Ranged 10, Intuition): 10/15/21 + Intuition damage; target is restrained (save ends). Ongoing 10 damage.
  • The Wode Remembers and Returns (9 Essence, Burst 4): Creates a persistent zone of difficult terrain; allies gain cover and begin recovering in the zone.
  • Heart of the Wode (11 Essence): Summons a size-5 tree. Allies adjacent can Catch Breath as a free maneuver; enemies adjacent are restrained.
  • The Green Defends Its Servants (11 Essence, Maneuver): Grants 30 temporary stamina; the shield explodes when depleted, dealing 10 damage.
  • O Flower Aid, O Earth Defend (5 Essence, Maneuver, Cube 3 within 10): Creates a persistent zone that heals allies, creates difficult terrain, and damages enemies.
  • So Gullible (5 Essence, Free Triggered): When a creature targets Gragnir, he teleports away and makes a free strike against the attacker.
  • Word of Guidance (Triggered): When an ally within 10 squares makes an ability roll, Gragnir grants them an edge.

Free Strike: Hurl Element (Ranged 10, Reason): Hurls elemental energy, choosing the damage type.

Negotiation: Interest 5 · Patience 5

Director Notes: Run Gragnir as a mentor who is protective but withholding. In combat, he is a devastating controller—Thorn Cage immobilizes priority targets, Heart of the Wode reshapes the battlefield, and So Gullible punishes anyone foolish enough to target him directly.

Key Trait: Gragnir will not do the work for the PCs. He points, he warns, he provides tools—but he will not save them.

Kaelen (The Seventh)

Role: Campaign emotional core, broken Ember-Keeper, reluctant guide

Appearance: Early twenties, ember-mark scar on forehead (faded but visible), haunted eyes, practical traveler’s gear.

Demeanor: Guarded, guilt-ridden, fiercely protective of common folk.

Backstory: Kaelen was the youngest Ember-Keeper ever chosen (age 13). She served faithfully for a year, then abandoned the order without explanation. The truth (GM secret, revealed in Phase 2): she broke guest-right, and the first draugr rose because of it.

Kaelen (The Seventh) — Draw Steel NPC

Level 5 Support Leader · EV 20 · Humanoid, Censor

Stamina: 80Speed: 5 (walk)Size: M
Stability: 1Free Strike: 5

Characteristics: Might +3 · Agility +1 · Reason +1 · Intuition +2 · Presence +4

Signature Abilities
  • Judgement (Maneuver, Ranged 10): You utter a prayer that outlines your foe in holy energy. The target is judged until end of encounter. Whenever the judged target uses a main action within Kaelen’s line of effect, she deals 2×Presence holy damage as a free triggered action. Spend 1 wrath to additionally: prevent shifts + melee free strike, impose a bane on the target’s power roll, reduce potency by 1, or taunt the target.
  • Protective Attack (Action, Melee, Might): Melee strike that taunts the target, drawing their attention away from allies.
Heroic Abilities
  • Righteous Judgment (9 Wrath, Melee): High-damage strike. When Kaelen hits a judged target, all allies who can see it can surge.
  • Edict of Stillness (7 Wrath, Maneuver): Creates an aura 2 zone around Kaelen. Enemies who leave the zone take holy damage.
  • Blessing of Steel (11 Wrath, Maneuver): Creates an aura 5 zone. Enemies take a bane on attacks; allies gain damage immunity 5.
  • Win This Day! (9 Wrath, Burst 3): All allies in burst surge 2, spend a recovery, and remove one “save ends” condition.

Triggered: Parry — When hit, Kaelen shifts 1 square and takes half damage; reduces potency.

Negotiation: Interest 5 · Patience 4
Motivations: Redemption, Duty, Protecting common folk
Pitfalls: Glory, Orders from authority, Minimizing her guilt

Director Notes: Play Kaelen as gruff, cynical, and duty-bound. She helps because she must, not because she believes she deserves redemption. In combat, Kaelen is a force multiplier—Judgement punishes a single priority target relentlessly while Win This Day! can swing a losing fight. Her Negotiation stats matter: convincing Kaelen to return is a major challenge in Phase 2. Appeal to duty and redemption; avoid invoking authority or glory.

Key Trait: Kaelen’s guilt is Svellheim-native. She broke an oath, and reality broke with it.

Llewyellen “Lew” Tosferd (The Finder)

Role: Practical miracle-worker, information broker, cost-accountant

Appearance: Middle-aged, weathered, always smiling (even when the smile doesn’t reach his eyes).

Demeanor: Friendly, transactional, never offers anything for free.

Function: Lew can find things that are lost—including people. He uses a mix of old contracts, informant networks, and (maybe) subtle magic he won’t admit to.

Llewyellen “Lew” Tosferd — Draw Steel NPC

Level 4 Support Standard · EV 16 · Humanoid, Shadow

Stamina: 60Speed: 6 (walk)Size: M
Stability: 0Free Strike: 4

Characteristics: Might +0 · Agility +3 · Reason +2 · Intuition +4 · Presence +3

Features
  • Crafty: Lew doesn’t provoke opportunity attacks by moving.
  • Keep It Down (Shadow 4th-level): While conversing with someone who shares his language, Lew decides whether anyone else can perceive what he’s conveying—even while yelling or in a crowded room.
  • Smoke Bomb (Shadow / Caustic Alchemy): Lew can use the Hide maneuver even while observed and without cover or concealment. He shifts 3 squares (Agility), and if he ends with cover or concealment, he is automatically hidden.
Signature Abilities
  • I’m No Threat (Maneuver, Magic / Harlequin Mask): You envelop yourself in an illusion that makes you appear nonthreatening and harmless. Strikes gain an edge; +1 bonus distance to Disengage. Ends when Lew harms a creature. Spend 1 insight: appear as a specific creature within 10 squares with an edge on tests to pass as them.
  • Careful Observation (Maneuver, Ranged 20): Assess a target. As long as Lew maintains line of effect and doesn’t strike another creature first, his next strike gains an edge and 1 surge.
Heroic Abilities
  • Clever Trick (1 Insight, Triggered, Magic): Trigger: An enemy targets Lew with a strike. Choose an enemy within the strike’s distance (including the attacker). The strike targets that enemy instead.
  • So Gullible (5 Insight, Free Triggered, Magic): Trigger: A creature targets Lew with a strike. Use Clever Trick for free, teleport 3 squares, make a free strike, and spend a Recovery.
  • You Talk Too Much (9 Insight, Melee 1 or Ranged 5, Agility): 10/15/21 + Agility damage; target is dazed (save ends, Presence potency) and can’t communicate with anyone until end of encounter.

Negotiation: Interest 6 · Patience 5
Motivations: Profit, Curiosity, Self-preservation
Pitfalls: Threats, Moralizing, Demanding charity

Director Notes: Lew’s Negotiation stats matter more than his combat stats—the PCs need to bargain with him, not fight him. If cornered, Lew uses Smoke Bomb to vanish, Crafty to move without provoking, and Clever Trick / So Gullible to punish anyone foolish enough to swing at him. He does not stand and fight. Use Keep It Down for covert tavern briefings and I’m No Threat for infiltration scenes.

Key Trait: Lew always names his price. And he always collects.

Mechanics to Use (GM)

Heat, Weight, and Word (Campaign Currencies)

Svellheim’s economy runs on three currencies:

  • Heat: Fuel, food, shelter, warmth. Measured in “ration-days” or “lantern-hours.”
  • Weight: Tools, weapons, repairs, transportation. Measured in iron-tokens, cart-days, or smith-hours.
  • Word: Reputation, contracts, oaths, legal standing. Measured in testimonies, warrants, or Thing-votes.

Use these as costs for Montage Tests, Negotiation leverage, and faction interactions.

Montage Tests: Travel as Consequences (Draw Steel, p. 261)

Every travel leg between major locations is a Montage Test. The heroes take turns making tests as they tackle the journey together across rounds. Each hero acts once per round (roll a test, assist another hero’s test, or use an ability/item). A hero cannot use the same skill twice across the montage. Two montage test rounds, then the test ends.

Montage Test Difficulty (base: 5 heroes)
DifficultySuccess LimitFailure Limit
Easy55
Moderate64
Hard73

Adjustments: For 4 or fewer heroes, decrease both limits by 1 per hero fewer than 5 (minimum 2). For 6+, increase both by 1 per extra hero.

Outcomes

  • Total Success: Hit the success limit before the failure limit. Goal achieved without complication. Earn 1 Victory (easy/moderate) or 2 Victories (hard).
  • Partial Success: Hit the failure limit or run out of rounds, but have at least 2 more successes than failures. Succeed with a complication or cost. Earn 1 Victory (moderate/hard).
  • Total Failure: Hit the failure limit or run out of rounds without the 2-success buffer. The goal is not achieved.
Individual Test Outcomes (p. 250)
RollEasyMediumHard
≤ 11Success w/ consequenceFailureFailure w/ consequence
12–16SuccessSuccess w/ consequenceFailure
17+Success w/ rewardSuccessSuccess
Nat 19–20Success w/ rewardSuccess w/ rewardSuccess w/ reward

Skills grant +2 to the test (not an edge). Edges and banes apply as normal (+2/−2; double edge/bane shifts tier by 1).

Rewards and Consequences: Handle individually per test. Default rewards = hero tokens. Default consequences = the Director gains additional Malice in the next combat encounter.

Negotiation Encounters

Use Draw Steel’s Negotiation structure for:

  • Recruiting Kaelen (Phase 2) — Interest 5, Patience 4. Motivations: redemption, duty, protecting common folk. Pitfalls: glory, orders from authority, minimizing her guilt.
  • Bargaining with Lew (Phase 1) — Interest 6, Patience 5. Motivations: profit, curiosity, self-preservation. Pitfalls: threats, moralizing, demanding charity.
  • Gaining access to the Steam-Oasis (Phase 4)
  • Faction politics at the Thing (Phase 3)

Grafvitnir Pressure Check

After each travel montage or major faction scene, make a secret d6 check. This represents Grafvitnir’s network acting on intelligence relayed by Dreyfus from the king’s side:

  • 1–3: No network activity this phase. Intelligence is delayed or agents are out of position.
  • 4–5: Grafvitnir’s agents execute a complication: a social obstacle (warrant, testimony, bribe from a corrupted official), a sabotage of Heat (spoiled stores, stolen lantern oil by Pale Maw operatives), or an ambush on bad footing (agents acting on Dreyfus’s intelligence).
  • 6: A Pale Maw agent or Wyrd-touched creature intercepts directly (ambush, forced confrontation, or dramatic reveal).

Adjust the die range as the campaign progresses. By Phase 4, Grafvitnir’s network should be almost certain to act.

Director Notes: How to Hide the True Mechanism

  • Let the world know there is a rite, but keep the steps contradictory.
  • Make every "source" incomplete or biased (temple wants control; old ways want payment; factions want leverage).
  • The Druid never lies, but also never gives the whole shape.

When the chosen PC learns the truth, it should feel like a key fitting a lock that has been in the campaign the whole time.
